items.q: Why are invasive species often more harmful than native species?
items.correct: They lack natural predators or competitors in their new environment, so they spread unchecked.
items.misconception: They are inherently more aggressive than native species.
items.random_wrong: They are larger than native species.
items.why: Students attribute success to species traits. The key factor is the ABSENCE OF CONTROLS - the same species may be ordinary in its native range, where co-evolved predators and competitors keep it in check.

items.q: Why are tropical rainforest soils often nutrient-poor despite abundant vegetation?
items.correct: Rapid decomposition and uptake mean nutrients cycle quickly through living biomass, not soil.
items.misconception: Rainforests have rich soils because they are productive.
items.random_wrong: Rainforests are deserts most of the year.
items.why: Students assume productive = rich soil. Actually most nutrients in rainforests are in living biomass; rapid uptake leaves soils thin. That's why clear-cutting destroys long-term productivity quickly.

items.q: Why is a cold winter not evidence against climate change?
items.correct: Climate refers to long-term averages, not individual weather events.
items.misconception: It is evidence - if it gets cold sometimes, warming must be wrong.
items.random_wrong: Cold winters are caused by warming.
items.why: Students conflate weather and climate. Climate is the LONG-TERM pattern (decades+); weather is short-term. Global warming refers to average trends - it allows individual cold events even as overall temperatures rise.

items.q: What acid forms when CO2 dissolves in seawater?
items.why: It's Carbonic acid. “Sulfuric acid” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Which material struggles to form in acidic oceans?
items.why: Correct answer: Calcium carbonate. “Silica gel” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Pre-industrial ocean pH was around 8.2. Today it is approximately what?
items.why: Exact answer: 8.1. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
